g.data-narration: Welcome to Parallel Line Algorithms - essential techniques in computer graphics for drawing lines on raster displays.
Computer screens are made of pixels in a grid. We need algorithms to determine which pixels to light up for a straight line.
g.text: Why Do We Need Line Algorithms?

g.data-narration: Given two endpoints, we must find which grid positions best represent the mathematical line connecting them.
DDA samples the line at regular intervals, calculating intermediate points by adding small increments to x and y coordinates.
g.text: DDA calculates the slope and steps through the major axis (axis with greater change):

g.data-narration: Let's trace DDA for line from (2,3) to (8,7). We step through x coordinates and calculate corresponding y values.
Bresenham's algorithm uses only integer arithmetic by comparing distances to choose the next pixel, making it extremely efficient.
g.text: Uses integer arithmetic only - the most widely used line drawing algorithm:
If P < 0: choose lower pixel | If P ≥ 0: choose upper pixel

g.data-narration: At each step, Bresenham compares two candidate pixels by checking which one is closer to the ideal line using the error term.
Tracing Bresenham for the same line (2,3) to (8,7): Initial P₀ = 2, we plot points based on whether P is negative or positive.
The Midpoint algorithm uses the implicit equation of a line to determine which pixel is closer to the ideal line.
g.text: Uses implicit line equation F(x,y) = Ax + By + C = 0 to make decisions:
A = y₁-y₂, B = x₂-x₁, C = x₁y₂ - x₂y₁

g.data-narration: The midpoint algorithm checks whether the midpoint between two candidate pixels lies above or below the line to make its choice.
When a line is steep (|dy| > |dx|), we step through y coordinates instead of x to maintain accuracy.
Lines can go in any direction - we handle negative slopes, right-to-left drawing, and swap axes when needed.
g.text: • Swap endpoints if x₂ < x₁ to ensure left-to-right

g.data-narration: Let's compare the three algorithms: DDA is simple but slow, Bresenham and Midpoint are fast with integer arithmetic.
Remember: All algorithms minimize error between ideal line and selected pixels. Bresenham dominates practical applications.
g.text: "Bresenham's algorithm is the industry standard for hardware implementations"
